speaker: interviewer
text: May 27, 1996. Survivor is Jacob Nagiel. Interviewer is Gaye Belfer. It is Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. The language is English. And this is tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Gaye Belfer, G-A-Y B-E-L-F-E-R. And I'm interviewing today Jacob Nagiel. The interview is taking place in Sydney, Australia. Today's date is May 27, 1996. And the language of the interview is English and maybe some Polish. OK. Could you say your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Jacob, J-A-C-O-B, Nagiel, N-A-G-I-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yankel, Yankel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Date of birth is 14/12/1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And can you tell me a little bit about your parents? Can you tell me their names and a little bit about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was Simon, and my mother was Helena. And I have two brothers and three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The names, say, my brother is Andrew. He's in the United States. The second brother is Moshe. He's in Tel Aviv, in Israel. And the three sisters, at the time of the war-- they-- they--

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they didn't survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want the name of my sisters?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is Fela, the oldest; Pola, the younger; and Ala, the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what sort of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a merchant in-- in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the family well off?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends on the-- on the-- OK, on the time. Sometimes yes, sometimes worse or sometimes better, like any business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born in 1912?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then was the First World War. Did you ever hear anything about those times in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember where we lived close to-- to the-- to the River Vistula. And I remember some-- some-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] some cartridges, cartridges fell through the window in-- in the-- in the-- in the wardrobes. And for a long time, I remember one country, it was in a [INAUDIBLE] in state, so it was long time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that hard times for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, it was hard times, yes, for everybody, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And tell me a little bit about your home life. Were your family religious or traditional?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traditional, but not very religious. But we-- we used to-- in the-- in the house we used to eat kosher, of course. And my mother was every Friday bench the-- the candles. And my father went to synagogue, yes. He used to take us too, of course, we were smaller. When we were older, we-- we didn't attend much synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And did you have any other family? Did you have grandparents and aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I have grandparents from Father's side, and-- and from the mother's side, and uncles, yes. A lot of uncles, yes and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all lived in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mostly Warsaw. But some lived in Otwock, not far from Warsaw. And some immigrated to-- to Belgium, where they meet in Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your home address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. It was-- the last letters were [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What number?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always Polish, of course, Polish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- did-- did the parents speak Yiddish at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father speak Yiddish. My mother speak Yiddish. But it was not a perfect Yiddish. Later on, when I work for an organization but before the war, I learned Yiddish, right Yiddish. More-- more literate Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reading and writing, more--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, not writing, but only reading--

speaker: interviewer
text: Only reading.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- only talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only talking, yes. But more correct Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: More correct, Yiddish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and did you ever come across any antisemitism while you were growing up in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the-- it's the way of life. It was normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, any incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no special incidents, but always, Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH], and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Calling names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not especially, sometimes yes, but not-- not too much. Because we-- we were mostly lived within Jewish children, and-- and families. So we don't meet much Polish people, only in the schools, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did you go to a-- a Jewish school or to a Polish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, their Polish school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the primary?

speaker: interviewee
text: The primary, Polish school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was many Jewish children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And did you have some Jewish education as well? Did you have-- go to cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much, very little. Yes, I-- I-- I attended cheder for a few months or something, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you had a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, I have a bar mitzvah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after the primary school, did you go to a-- a high school, a gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. In Otwock. I have some-- an aunt in Otwock, and I was living there a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Otwock is not far from Warsaw. It is 30 or 35 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you go there for high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is-- this is-- of course they had me because my family was six children. And my-- my aunt was my-- my mother's sister. There was only two. So they-- [LAUGHS] they take me to help, to help the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what sort of a high school was it? Was it a-- a government school or a private--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a government school, yes. It's what they call [NON-ENGLISH]. It means there's girls and boys in one school together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you learn other languages when you were there? Did you learn German, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I learn a little French, but-- I don't remember much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did you like to do? Did you have any hobbies or interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I was always interested in in-- [? push ?] [? walking, ?] and-- and bike riding, and horse riding, and-- and being in the fields, in the woods, and this was my life. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You like-- you like the nature?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, very much, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And animals?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Animals too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Animals, did you like animals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, I love very much, horses and dogs. I always had a dog, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And was this unusual for-- for Jewish people to have this interest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- was-- yes, it was not very common, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know where that interest comes from?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the only-- were the only one in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were in the high school, at your aunt's place, did you have contact with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It's not far.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But you lived there? You lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what did you do when you finished high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attend the agriculture school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. This was-- this was in Warsaw. And it was-- it was more antisemitic than other schools. Because the students were antisemetic. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they-- what did they do? What-- what do you remember from that antisemitism-- about that antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just they don't want to sit together. They don't want to sit together on the bench or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but many didn't know I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there boys and girls at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just boys. Was it a-- like a tertiary institute? Was it like-- like the status of a university?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose so, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a technical school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what did you study there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's a very big-- big program studying. We studying about-- about all-- everything what is from the land from the-- from everything. Starting from-- from-- I couldn't tell you in-- in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it farming, farming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Farming, yes. But farming in-- in all directions, and not-- not specialized in one direction, but everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: To do-- to do with crops, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: With crops, with cattle, with-- with-- with-- I-- I couldn't tell you. With all branches would come from-- from the land. Because farming in this time means a mixed farm with everything. A farm has cows, and has horses, and has woods, and has corn, and has vegetable, everything. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And how long did you study at that college?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and when-- when was it when you graduated, what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated in-- what was it? [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I suppose it was '32 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find a job straight away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I find a job. It-- it's easy for me to find a job, because with many Jewish land owners, and they're always looking for-- for a Jewish agronomist. And there was not many agronomists Jewish, so I-- I have very easy to find a job, and I was working in a few places, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first job after--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working for-- for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a man, Kohn, Oskar Kohn. It's a big factory, but say, half a big farm, very close to the factory, a very big farm in-- in-- in Lodz. And I was working there and even there was a kibbutz was there prepare for aliyah to-- to Israel. And I teach them to-- to-- to-- to work in the garden, and to work in the fields, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's that what they call hakhsharot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Haksharot. Yes, it was a hakhsharot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And you said before, you were the only one in the family interested in agriculture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your brothers do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was electrician, Andrew. And-- and Moshe was a bookkeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they also worked in-- in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sisters, they-- they attend school, and-- and was at home. They're not married. No, not married. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And-- can you tell me a little bit about your life in Lodz when you moved to Lodz to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I work for-- for Kohn in the-- in the-- in the farm. And it was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a children-- a children home. They have a big garden. And they-- the [? mind-- ?] the-- the director of the home was a man known now in the-- in the Jewish community, Rumkowski. Rumkowski was very, very famous at this time. I think he-- he later on was in the-- in the occupation, he was from the-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat, he was head of the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the ghetto, in the ghetto in-- in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was not at this time in Lodz. But yes, I hear about him, yes, a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of man was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very-- a very-- [POLISH]. I don't know the word.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: False? False?

speaker: interviewee
text: False. He tells something, and he means something else. And I remember the sisters who are working for him and they-- and they say always when people are coming, because the visitors would-- would give money for the children-- he always take two children and keeps them. [LAUGHS] And when it's time to-- the children doesn't know when it's time to-- to leave. And he start to-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pinch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pinch them to go away. [LAUGHS] And he would take them, and he-- he [INAUDIBLE], yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wasn't such a nice man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very nasty. He was not a nice man.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he promise me so much when I come over, to-- I build glass houses there, and I-- and I make a-- some vegetables for the all in the fields. And the children, I teach them how to work in the gardens. And I teach them. It was very-- he-- he promise me everything that I come over. And I didn't-- I didn't work long there, and I go another place, another place was-- was a Yiddish institution. It was [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. And they have a sanitorium for-- for people-- for-- for all the people, not children, for all the people there was by [? IT, ?] [? IT ?] re-convalescent. Like a convalescent home. And-- and it was a big farm, with cows and horses, and-- and-- and corn field. This was a big farm, and I was the head of the-- of the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was-- that was just near Lodz? Was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was near Lodz, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The [INAUDIBLE] place was near Lodz, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and just going back to Rumkowski, the children's-- was-- was that an orphanage? Was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: An orphanage, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and do you know what happened to those children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- I would say, tell what I hear. And later on, he take-- he took them to-- to the chamber somewhere, I don't know. Rumkowski, I saw-- I think a picture when-- in a newspaper or somewhere [? I remember ?] or in a film, or like a documentary film. And when he-- he walked with the children to the chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit more about the hospital, the sanatorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: The sanatorium, it was a-- a nice building, and Jewish-- all Jewish workers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, there was a good friend of mine, he was the electrician. Because they have, they have a-- a generator to run for the electricity on the whole farm. His name was Kanisczer. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the patients all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all Jewish. All Jewish. It was for Jewish and-- and from Jewish people, the rich people in Lodz, where they-- where they--

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where they give money for this, only Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including, was that including Oskar Kohn? Did he give money for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I suppose anybody, yes. Because Lodz was very much, very rich Jewish factories, very rich people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. They worked-- did they work in the textile industry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kohn was textile industry, the biggest I think who was in Poland in this time. We call this [NON-ENGLISH]. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Somebody told me the son, Oskar, went somewhere into Sweden or somewhere. Because he was a Swedish consulate before in-- in Poland. Yes. But what's happened further, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And around this time, Hitler was coming to power in Germany. Did you know about that? Did you read about it, and hear about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We read about it, and know about it. And many Jews from Germany run to Poland. And when I was in Germany later on, they told me there-- there was not a good invitation from the Polish Jews for the German Jews, who have some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Polish-- in Polish, say it in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH] They are not-- not satisfied with the handling, the-- the German Jews when they come from Germany to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't-- they weren't welcome?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not very good, not very welcome. Yes. This is what they told me when I was in Germany later on. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you come across any of those refugees when you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. And what was happening in Poland in those years, in the 1930s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special, what was happening I remember, it was the-- the Hitler-- I don't remember his name. They were there in Poland-- they were-- they were hunting in Poland. This I remember. But for a time, until the-- the '39, when the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were some-- were some Polish Jews trying to get away? Were any of them leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose so, yes. Yes. I mean up here one, a local, who run from Poland before the-- the occupation. But he-- he passed away last year, Diamond Hirsch. He told me he-- he went to France before. And he told me, I was [INAUDIBLE]. He run away earlier though.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what happened with your brothers? Did they stay in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Moshe was from 1933 in Israel, so he was not up here. He was in the English army. And in the English army, takes some-- take the Jewish boys to the army. Because it was under occupation, English occupation. And he was in the English army. He was in Rome. He was in Egypt in the English army. And later he was in Tel Aviv and married in Tel Aviv, and stayed in Tel Aviv for all that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Andrew? He stayed in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrew stayed in Poland, because he was in the army, in the same time that I was in the army. He was in the front. And when the Polish army was destroyed and they give up the-- the-- the-- their arms, they give up everything. So he went to-- to Lvov. He went to Lvov. And when the-- when the German take the-- when start the-- the-- the German-Russian war start in '40-- during '42 I suppose, '42, he run further to further deep in Russia. And he was there alone in Cheliabinsk where we are meeting later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how he survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And tell me. Did you ever travel very much in that time before the war started? Did you travel outside of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not much. Because it was very dear, and if you call it. No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you come across any personal attacks or any antisemitism in your work? Did you ever come across incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not especially, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And just up until the war came in 1939, what was the last job you were doing before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before they war, I was working for a Jewish-- sanatorium. We call this a sanatorium. This was for children, a sanatorium. This was run by the Bundist organization. This was [INAUDIBLE], not far from Warsaw. And this was the nicest time of my life. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a good place to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And were you-- were you a member of the Bund?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not a member, no. But I was treated very good there. Yes, I meet my wife there, and I married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked there, yes. She was a dietitian, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you meet any of the-- the Bundists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I meet-- I meet the Bundist is fair and the Bundist society they must be famous, Dr. Ivinska. Because she comes-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she comes from Belgium. And I think the headquarters, of Bundist headquarters must be in Belgium, in Brussels. And she come very often. And she visits the sanatorium. Yes, she took some pictures for me too, when I was in Brussels, I just forgot about it. I visited her after the war, I visit her. Yes. Thank you. [DRINKS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And do you remember the names of any of the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was Gilinski. He was the head-- the head-- the head director, I don't know, and Brumberg, and Lazar Gilinskibotke.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and where did the money come for that hospital? Who supported that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a hospital. It's a sanatorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sanatorium, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think from Bundist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: All over the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And-- and were you were a Zionist in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was a leftist. I was a socialist.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't think of going to live in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I didn't, think, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even when you were-- were training the people on the hakhsharot, you didn't think of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. No. I make a mistake. [LAUGHS] I should at this time leave Poland. Yes, they ask me to come with them. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and what was the reason that you didn't go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know myself, some-- I don't know. I was not ready to go. I don't know myself. I couldn't explain this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did-- you said you met your wife at the sanitarium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anya.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married in-- in-- oh what was the dates-- in '30-- '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, '38 maybe '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Start before-- it's just before the war started, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you live once you got married? Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We live in Warsaw. We live in Warsaw, close to the-- to the-- to the hospital of [INAUDIBLE]. It's a hospital we called [INAUDIBLE] Hospital. On a street [INAUDIBLE]. And we start to-- to build up a home just for a very short time, and then everything went wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me what happened just when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started, when I have to go, yes. The first, when I-- when I-- they collect the old soldiers, the German, the German collect the soldiers, what they need. But I was in Prague from [INAUDIBLE] the-- the-- the river in Prague. And I have to go to Warsaw. I have to go through the bridge, the [INAUDIBLE] Bridge. And they're standing on the bridge, and every soldier they collect, and they put in a-- in some-- in some [INAUDIBLE], I don't how to call this. In some place when it's first a fence around. And they put them in, and they keep them there. And when I was in the-- in the-- in the place where they put me, and there was a lot of soldiers. And we started to talk what they are going to do to us. And see, mostly say they take us to Germany to-- to-- to dig potatoes or something. And-- and this was a time when I-- when I decide to run away. So I jumped the fence, and-- and run away, and take off the-- the-- [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: The uniform, yes. And-- and went home, and went home. And the home was-- the whole house was bombarded. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. There was nobody there.
